Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## DATASET CONDENSATION WITH GRADIENT MATCHING

## **Bo Zhao, Konda Reddy Mopuri, Hakan Bilen**

School of Informatics, The University of Edinburgh _{_ bo.zhao, kmopuri, hbilen _}_ @ed.ac.uk

## ABSTRACT

As the state-of-the-art machine learning methods in many fields rely on larger datasets, storing datasets and training models on them become significantly more expensive. This paper proposes a training set synthesis technique for _data-efficient_ learning, called _Dataset Condensation_ , that learns to condense large dataset into a small set of informative synthetic samples for training deep neural networks from scratch. We formulate this goal as a gradient matching problem between the gradients of deep neural network weights that are trained on the original and our synthetic data. We rigorously evaluate its performance in several computer vision benchmarks and demonstrate that it significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art methods[1] . Finally we explore the use of our method in continual learning and neural architecture search and report promising gains when limited memory and computations are available.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Large-scale datasets, comprising millions of samples, are becoming the norm to obtain state-ofthe-art machine learning models in multiple fields including computer vision, natural language processing and speech recognition. At such scales, even storing and preprocessing the data becomes burdensome, and training machine learning models on them demands for specialized equipment and infrastructure. An effective way to deal with large data is data selection – identifying the most representative training samples – that aims at improving _data efficiency_ of machine learning techniques. While classical data selection methods, also known as coreset construction (Agarwal et al., 2004; Har-Peled & Mazumdar, 2004; Feldman et al., 2013), focus on clustering problems, recent work can be found in continual learning (Rebuffi et al., 2017; Toneva et al., 2019; Castro et al., 2018; Aljundi et al., 2019) and active learning (Sener & Savarese, 2018) where there is typically a fixed budget in storing and labeling training samples respectively. These methods commonly first define a criterion for representativeness ( _e.g_ . in terms of compactness (Rebuffi et al., 2017; Castro et al., 2018), diversity (Sener & Savarese, 2018; Aljundi et al., 2019), forgetfulness (Toneva et al., 2019)), then select the representative samples based on the criterion, finally use the selected small set to train their model for a downstream task.

Unfortunately, these methods have two shortcomings: they typically rely on i) heuristics ( _e.g_ . picking cluster centers) that does not guarantee any optimal solution for the downstream task ( _e.g_ . image classification), ii) presence of representative samples, which is neither guaranteed. A recent method, Dataset Distillation (DD) (Wang et al., 2018) goes beyond these limitations by _learning_ a small set of informative images from large training data. In particular, the authors model the network parameters as a function of the synthetic training data and learn them by minimizing the training loss over the original training data w.r.t. synthetic data. Unlike in the coreset methods, the synthesized data are directly optimized for the downstream task and thus the success of the method does not rely on the presence of representative samples.

Inspired from DD (Wang et al., 2018), we focus on learning to _synthesize informative samples_ that are optimized to train neural networks for downstream tasks and not limited to individual samples in original dataset. Like DD, our goal is to obtain the highest generalization performance with a model trained on a small set of synthetic images, ideally comparable performance to that of a model trained on the original images (see Figure 1(a)). In particular, we investigate the following

questions. Is it possible to i) compress a large image classification dataset into a small synthetic set, ii) train an image classification model on the synthetic set that can be further used to classify real images, iii) learn a single set of synthetic images that can be used to train different neural network architectures? To this end, we propose a _Dataset Condensation_ method to learn a small set of “condensed” synthetic samples such that a deep neural network trained on them obtains not only similar performance but also a close solution to a network trained on the large training data in the network parameter space. We formulate this goal as a minimization problem between two sets of gradients of the network parameters that are computed for a training loss over a large fixed training set and a learnable condensed set (see Figure 1(b)). We show that our method enables effective learning of synthetic images and neural networks trained on them, outperforms (Wang et al., 2018) and coreset methods with a wide margin in multiple computer vision benchmarks. In addition, learning a compact set of synthetic samples also benefits other learning problems when there is a fixed budget on training images. We show that our method outperforms popular data selection methods by providing more informative training samples in continual learning. Finally, we explore a promising use case of our method in neural architecture search, and show that – once our condensed images are learned – they can be used to train numerous network architectures extremely efficiently.

Our method is related to knowledge distillation (KD) techniques (Hinton et al., 2015; Buciluˇa et al., 2006; Ba & Caruana, 2014; Romero et al., 2014) that transfer the knowledge in an ensemble of models to a single one. Unlike KD, we distill knowledge of a large training set into a small synthetic set. Our method is also related to Generative Adversarial Networks (Goodfellow et al., 2014a; Mirza & Osindero, 2014; Radford et al., 2015) and Variational AutoEncoders (Kingma & Welling, 2013) that synthesize high-fidelity samples by capturing the data distribution. In contrast, our goal is to generate informative samples for training deep neural networks rather than to produce “real-looking” samples. Finally our method is related to the methods that produce image patches by projecting the feature activations back to the input pixel space (Zeiler & Fergus, 2014), reconstruct the input image by matching the feature activations (Mahendran & Vedaldi, 2015), recover private training images for given training gradients (Zhu et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2020), synthesize features from semantic embeddings for zero-shot learning (Sariyildiz & Cinbis, 2019). Our goal is however to synthesize a set of condensed training images not to recover the original or missing training images.

In the remainder of this paper, we first review the problem of dataset condensation and introduce our method in section 2, present and analyze our results in several image recognition benchmarks in section 3.1, showcase applications in continual learning and network architecture search in section 3.2, and conclude the paper with remarks for future directions in section 4.

In practice, _**θ**[T]_ for different initializations can be trained first in an offline stage and then used as the target parameter vector in eq. (5). However, there are two potential issues by learning to regress _**θ**[T]_ as the target vector. First the distance between _**θ**[T]_ and intermediate values of _**θ**[S]_ can be too big in the parameter space with multiple local minima traps along the path and thus it can be too challenging to reach. Second opt-alg involves a limited number of optimization steps as a tradeoff between speed and accuracy which may not be sufficient to take enough steps for reaching the optimal solution. These problems are similar to those of (Wang et al., 2018), as they both involve parameterizing _**θ**[S]_ with _S_ and _**θ**_ 0.

**Discussion.** The synthetic data contains not only samples but also their labels ( _**s** , y_ ) that can be jointly learned by optimizing eq. (9) in theory. **Algorithm.** We depict the optimization details in Alg. 1. At the outer level, it contains a loop over random weight initializations, as we want to obtain condensed images that can later be used to train previously unseen models. Once _**θ**_ is randomly initialized, we use _φ_ _**θ**_ to first compute the loss over both the training samples ( _L[T]_ ), synthetic samples ( _L[S]_ ) and their gradients w.r.t. _**θ**_ , then optimize the synthetic samples _S_ to match these gradients _∇_ _**θ** L[S]_ to _∇_ _**θ** L[T]_ by applying _ςS_ gradient descent steps with learning rate _ηS_ . We use the stochastic gradient descent optimization for both opt-alg _**θ**_ and opt-alg _S_ . Next we train _**θ**_ on the updated synthetic images by minimizing the loss _L[S]_ with learning rate _η_ _**θ**_ for _ς_ _**θ**_ steps. Note that we sample each real and synthetic batch pair from _T_ and _S_ containing samples from a single class and the synthetic data for each class are separately (or parallelly) updated at each iteration ( _t_ ) for the following reasons: i) this reduces memory use at train time, ii) imitating the mean gradients w.r.t. the data from single class is easier compared to those of multiple classes. This does not bring any extra computational cost.

In contrast to (Lopez-Paz et al., 2017; Aljundi et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019) that ignore the layer-wise structure by flattening tensors over all layers to one vector and then computing the distance between two vectors, we group them for each output node. We found that this is a better distance for gradient matching (see the supplementary) and enables using a single learning rate across all layers.

## 3 EXPERIMENTS

## 3.1 DATASET CONDENSATION

First we evaluate classification performance with the condensed images on four standard benchmark datasets: digit recognition on MNIST (LeCun et al., 1998), SVHN (Netzer et al., 2011) and object classification on FashionMNIST (Xiao et al., 2017), CIFAR10 (Krizhevsky et al., 2009). We test our method using six standard deep network architectures: MLP, ConvNet (Gidaris & Komodakis, 2018), LeNet (LeCun et al., 1998), AlexNet (Krizhevsky et al., 2012), VGG-11 (Simonyan & Zisserman, 2014) and ResNet-18 (He et al., 2016). MLP is a multilayer perceptron with two nonlinear hidden layers, each has 128 units. ConvNet is a commonly used modular architecture in few-shot

learning (Snell et al., 2017; Vinyals et al., 2016; Gidaris & Komodakis, 2018) with _D_ duplicate blocks, and each block has a convolutional layer with _W_ (3 _×_ 3) filters, a normalization layer _N_ , an activation layer _A_ and a pooling layer _P_ , denoted as [ _W, N, A, P_ ] _× D_ . The default ConvNet (unless specified otherwise) includes 3 blocks, each with 128 filters, followed by InstanceNorm (Ulyanov et al., 2016), ReLU and AvgPooling modules. The final block is followed by a linear classifier. We use Kaiming initialization (He et al., 2015) for network weights. The synthetic images can be initialized from Gaussian noise or randomly selected real training images. More details about the datasets, networks and hyper-parameters can be found in the supplementary.

The pipeline for dataset condensation has two stages: learning the condensed images (denoted as C) and training classifiers from scratch on them (denoted as T). Note that the model architectures used in two stages might be different. For the coreset baselines, the coreset is selected in the first stage. We investigate three settings: 1, 10 and 50 image/class learning, which means that the condensed set or coreset contains 1, 10 and 50 images per class respectively. Each method is run for 5 times, and 5 synthetic sets are generated in the first stage; each generated synthetic set is used to train 20 randomly initialized models in the second stage and evaluated on the test set, which amounts to evaluating 100 models in the second stage. In all experiments, we report the mean and standard deviation of these 100 testing results.

**Baselines.** We compare our method to four coreset baselines (Random, Herding, K-Center and Forgetting) and also to DD (Wang et al., 2018). In Random, the training samples are randomly selected as the coreset. Herding baseline, which selects closest samples to the cluster center, is based on (Welling, 2009) and used in (Rebuffi et al., 2017; Castro et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Belouadah & Popescu, 2020). K-Center (Wolf, 2011; Sener & Savarese, 2018) picks multiple center points such that the largest distance between a data point and its nearest center is minimized. For Herding and K-Center, we use models trained on the whole dataset to extract features, compute _l_ 2 distance to centers. Forgetting method (Toneva et al., 2019) selects the training samples which are easy to forget during training. We do not compare to GSS-Greedy (Aljundi et al., 2019), because it is also a similarity based greedy algorithm like K-Center, but GSS-Greedy trains an online learning model to measure the similarity of samples, which is different from general image classification problem. More detailed comparisons can be found in the supplementary.

**Comparison to coreset methods.** We first compare our method to the coreset baselines on MNIST, FashionMNIST, SVHN and CIFAR10 in Table 1 using the default ConvNet in classification accuracy. Whole dataset indicates training on the whole original set which serves as an approximate upper-bound performance. First we observe that our method outperforms all the baselines significantly and achieves a comparable result (98 _._ 8%) in case of 50 images per class to the upper bound (99 _._ 6%) in MNIST which uses two orders of magnitude more training images per class (6000). We also obtain promising results in FashionMNIST, however, the gap between our method and upper bound is bigger in SVHN and CIFAR10 which contain more diverse images with varying foregrounds and backgrounds. We also observe that, (i) the random selection baseline is competitive to other coreset methods in 10 and 50 images per class and (ii) herding method is on average the best coreset technique. We visualize the condensed images produced by our method under 1 image/class setting in Figure 2. Interestingly they are interpretable and look like “prototypes” of each class.

**Comparison to DD (Wang et al., 2018).** Unlike the setting in Table 1, DD (Wang et al., 2018) reports results only for 10 images per class on MNIST and CIFAR10 over LeNet and AlexCifarNet (a customized AlexNet). We strictly follow the experimental setting in (Wang et al., 2018), use the same architectures and report our and their original results in Table 3 for a fair comparison. Our method achieves significantly better performance than DD on both benchmarks; obtains 5% higher accuracy with only 1 synthetic sample per class than DD with 10 samples per class. In addition, our method obtains consistent results over multiple runs with a standard deviation of only 0 _._ 6% on MNIST, while DD’s performance significantly vary over different runs (8 _._ 1%). Finally our method trains 2 times faster than DD and requires 50% less memory on CIFAR10 experiments. More detailed runtime and qualitative comparison can be found in the supplementary.

**Cross-architecture generalization.** Another key advantage of our method is that the condensed images learned using one architecture can be used to train another unseen one. Here we learn 1 condensed image per class for MNIST over a diverse set of networks including MLP, ConvNet (Gidaris & Komodakis, 2018), LeNet (LeCun et al., 1998), AlexNet (Krizhevsky et al., 2012), VGG-11 (Simonyan & Zisserman, 2014) and ResNet-18 (He et al., 2016) (see Table 2). Once the condensed sets are synthesized, we train every network on all the sets separately from scratch and evaluate their cross architecture performance in terms of classification accuracy on the MNIST test set. Table 2 shows that the condensed images, especially the ones that are trained with convolutional networks, perform well and are thus architecture generic. MLP generated images do not work well for training convolutional architectures which is possibly due to the mismatch between translation invariance properties of MLP and convolutional networks. Interestingly, MLP achieves better performance with convolutional network generated images than the MLP generated ones. The best results are obtained in most cases with ResNet generated images and ConvNet or ResNet as classifiers which is inline with their performances when trained on the original dataset.

**Number of condensed images.** We also study the test performance of a ConvNet trained on them for MNIST, FashionMNIST, SVHN and CIFAR10 for various number of condensed images per class in Figure 3 in absolute and relative terms – normalized by its upper-bound. Increasing the number of condensed images improves the accuracies in all benchmarks and further closes the gap with the upper-bound performance especially in MNIST and FashionMNIST, while the gap remains larger in SVHN and CIFAR10. In addition, our method outperforms the coreset method - Herding by a large margin in all cases.

**Activation, normalization & pooling.** We also study the effect of various activation (sigmoid, ReLU (Nair & Hinton, 2010; Zeiler et al., 2013), leaky ReLU (Maas et al., 2013)), pooling (max, average) and normalization functions (batch (Ioffe & Szegedy, 2015), group (Wu & He, 2018), layer (Ba et al., 2016), instance norm (Ulyanov et al., 2016)) and have the following observations: i) leaky ReLU over ReLU and average pooling over max pooling enable learning better condensed images, as they allow for denser gradient flow; ii) instance normalization obtains better classifica-

## 3.2 APPLICATIONS

**Continual Learning** First we apply our method to a continual-learning scenario (Rebuffi et al., 2017; Castro et al., 2018) where new tasks are learned incrementally and the goal is to preserve the performance on the old tasks while learning the new ones. We build our model on E2E method in (Castro et al., 2018) that uses a limited budget rehearsal memory (we consider 10 images/class here) to keep representative samples from the old tasks and knowledge distillation (KD) to regularize the network’s output w.r.t. to previous predictions. We replace its sample selection mechanism (herding) with ours such that a set of condensed images are generated and stored in the memory, keep the rest of the model same and evaluate this model on the task-incremental learning problem on the digit recognition datasets, SVHN (Netzer et al., 2011), MNIST (LeCun et al., 1998) and USPS (Hull, 1994) in the same order. MNIST and USPS images are reshaped to 32 _×_ 32 RGB images.

We compare our method to E2E (Castro et al., 2018), depicted as herding in Figure 4, with and without KD regularization. The experiment contains 3 incremental training stages (SVHN _→_ MNIST _→_ USPS) and testing accuracies are computed by averaging over the test sets of the previous and current tasks after each stage. The desired outcome is to obtain high mean classification accuracy at T3. The results indicate that the condensed images are more _data-efficient_ than the ones sampled by herding and thus our method outperforms E2E in both settings, while by a larger margin (2 _._ 3% at T3) when KD is not employed.

**Neural Architecture Search.** Here we explore the use of our method in a simple neural architecture search (NAS) experiment on CIFAR10 which typically requires expensive training of numerous architectures multiple times on the whole training set and picking the best performing ones on a validation set. Our goal is to verify that our condensed images can be used to efficiently train multiple networks to identify the best network. To this end, we construct the search space of 720 ConvNets as described in Section 3.1 by varying hyper-parameters _W_ , _N_ , _A_ , _P_ , _D_ over an uniform grid (see supplementary for more details), train them for 100 epochs on three small proxy datasets (10 images/class) that are obtained with Random sampling, Herding and our method. Note that we train the condensed images for once only with the default ConvNet architecture and use them to train all kinds of architectures. We also compare to early-stopping (Li & Talwalkar, 2020) in which the model is trained on whole training set but with the same number of training iterations as the one required for the small proxy datasets, in other words, for the same amount of computations.

Table 4 depicts i) the average test performance of the best selected model over 5 runs when trained on the whole dataset, ii) Spearman’s rank correlation coefficient between the validation accuracies obtained by training the selected top 10 models on the proxy dataset and whole dataset, iii) time for training 720 architectures on a NVIDIA GTX1080-Ti GPU, and iv) memory print of the training images. Our method achieves the highest testing performance (84.5%) and performance correlation (0.79), meanwhile significantly decreases the the searching time (from 8604.3 to 18.8 minutes) and storage space (from 5 _×_ 10[4] to 1 _×_ 10[2] images) compared to whole-dataset training. The competitive early-stopping baseline achieves on par performance for the best performing model with

Furthermore, early-stopping needs 100 times as many training images as ours needs. Note that the training time for synthetic images is around 50 minutes (for _K_ = 500) which is one time off and negligible cost when training thousands even millions of candidate architectures in NAS.

## 4 CONCLUSION

In this paper, we propose a dataset condensation method that learns to synthesize a small set of informative images. We show that these images are significantly more data-efficient than the same number of original images and the ones produced by the previous method, and they are not architecture dependent, can be used to train different deep networks. Once trained, they can be used to lower the memory print of datasets and efficiently train numerous networks which are crucial in continual learning and neural architecture search respectively. For future work, we plan to explore the use of condensed images in more diverse and thus challenging datasets like ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009) that contain higher resolution images with larger variations in appearance and pose of objects, background.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a novel and compelling approach to data-efficient learning through *Dataset Condensation*, framing the synthesis of informative training data as a gradient matching problem between networks trained on real and synthetic datasets. The core idea—aligning gradients from the original and condensed data to preserve learning dynamics—is conceptually elegant and well-motivated, particularly in contrast to heuristic-based data selection or coreset methods. The method is clearly positioned relative to prior work, including dataset distillation and knowledge distillation, with sufficient technical differentiation articulated. The writing is generally clear, and the high-level narrative—from problem formulation to applications in continual learning and architecture search—is logically structured and persuasive. However, the paper suffers from a critical shortcoming in exposition: the central *gradient matching* mechanism is introduced intuitively but lacks formal rigor in the provided text, with key mathematical details (likely due to truncation) missing or deferred. For instance, the loss function governing the synthesis of condensed data is referenced but not explicitly defined, undermining reproducibility and analytical clarity. Additionally, while the method claims broad applicability across architectures and tasks, the experimental scope appears limited in the presented portion, with insufficient detail on evaluation protocols, baseline comparisons, and ablation studies needed to fully substantiate the strong claims. The truncated presentation also leaves open whether implementation choices—such as network architecture, optimizer, or condition for gradient alignment—are sufficiently justified. A more complete theoretical grounding and transparent experimental design would be necessary to fully assess the validity and generalizability of the results. Nonetheless, the conceptual contribution is significant and the potential impact on efficient training, continual learning, and NAS is high, making this a promising work in need of stronger technical exposition.

comments.title: Gradient matching objective lacks justification for ignoring higher-order dynamics
comments.quote: We now have a single deep network with parameters **θ** trained on the synthetic set _S_ which is optimized such that the distance between the gradients for the loss over the training samples _L[T]_ w.r.t. **θ** and the gradients for the loss over the condensed samples _L[S]_ w.r.t. **θ** is minimized.
comments.explanation: The method proposes minimizing the difference between ∇_**θ** L[T] and ∇_**θ** L[S] as a proxy for aligning training dynamics. While this is intuitively appealing—matching gradients should lead to similar parameter updates—the paper assumes that first-order gradient alignment is sufficient to induce convergence to a similar solution in parameter space and generalization. However, in non-convex optimization landscapes typical of deep networks, matching gradients at a single point (especially early in training) does not guarantee convergence to a similar basin of attraction. The argument rests on local smoothness (cited from Rifai et al., 2012), but the paper lacks justification for why gradient matching at each step suffices to guide the entire optimization trajectory. Unlike full unrolling methods (e.g., Dataset Distillation), this approach avoids modeling long-range dependencies in the optimization path but provides no theoretical or empirical justification for this approximation. Strong experimental results do not compensate for the missing grounding in optimization theory or ablation (e.g., matching only at initialization or endpoint).

comments.title: Unjustified simplification from parameter convergence to gradient matching
comments.quote: Based on our observation ( _D_ ( **θ** t[S], **θ** t[T] ) ≈ 0 ), we simplify the formulation in eq. (7) by replacing **θ** t[T] with **θ** t[S] and use **θ** to denote **θ**[S] in the rest of the paper
comments.explanation: The derivation of gradient matching hinges on the assumption that **θ**_t[S] ≈ **θ**_t[T], which the authors claim is supported by preliminary experiments. However, this creates a circular argument, presupposing the method's success. The substitution collapses ∇_**θ** L[T](**θ**_t[T]) to ∇_**θ** L[T](**θ**_t[S]), relying on parameter proximity and smoothness, but without invoking a Taylor expansion or Lipschitz Hessian condition. This leap—from small parameter differences to gradient alignment—is non-trivial and insufficiently justified. While plausible under smoothness, it should be acknowledged as a meaningful approximation with potential error, rather than a simple substitution. Gradient matching appears heuristic rather than rigorously derived.

comments.title: Ambiguity in gradient matching across minibatches breaks symmetry assumption
comments.quote: we sample each real and synthetic batch pair from _T_ and _S_ containing samples from a single class and the synthetic data for each class are separately (or parallelly) updated at each iteration ( _t_ )
comments.explanation: The algorithm processes one class at a time and matches gradients computed on per-class real and synthetic minibatches. While beneficial for memory and empirical performance, this raises concerns: L[T] in line 7 is defined over single-class batches, whereas the reference model is trained on the full dataset with diverse minibatches. Gradients from single-class batches may carry class-specific biases (e.g., imbalance, co-activation patterns), and matching to such gradients could hinder generalization. The authors note that this imitates 'mean gradients' per class but do not justify why per-class alignment suffices to recover full multi-class dynamics. A comparison to mixed-class gradient matching (in supplementary or ablation) would have strengthened the design choice, which is currently underspecified and potentially misleading for replication.

comments.title: Ambiguous decomposition of parameter matching into iterative subproblems
comments.quote:
We can now decompose eq. (5) into multiple subproblems:

where _T_ is the number of iterations, _ρ[S]_ and _ρ[T]_ are the numbers of optimization steps for **θ**[S] and **θ**[T] respectively.
comments.explanation: The text introduces a decomposition of the parameter matching objective (eq. 5) into iterative subproblems involving **θ**_t[S] and **θ**_t[T], but no explicit equation is provided. It is unclear whether the objective minimizes D(**θ**_t[S], **θ**_t[T]) at each step and sums over t, or enforces sequential matching conditions. The omission of a formal equation (e.g., eq. 6) makes it hard to reconstruct the objective. While gradient matching is later introduced as an approximation, the intermediate conceptual step from global parameter matching to iterative decomposition remains ambiguous. A precise formulation would have improved rigor, especially for readers trying to understand the motivation behind gradient matching.

comments.title: Underspecified Synthetic Data Update in Algorithm 1
comments.explanation: The algorithm updates synthetic data _Sc_ by minimizing the gradient matching loss _D_, but the notation hides a non-trivial computational step: this requires computing ∇_S D, which involves second-order mixed derivatives (e.g., ∇_S ∇_**θ** L[S]_c). While the text notes SGD is used for opt-alg_S, it does not clarify that this is a higher-order gradient operation, which is critical for correct implementation. The omission may mislead readers into thinking only first-order gradients suffice, when in fact careful handling of ∇_S (∇_**θ** L[S]) is needed. This is a subtle implementation barrier not adequately addressed in the algorithmic description.

comments.title: Missing details on how cross-architecture evaluation is trained
comments.quote: Once the condensed sets are synthesized, we train every network on all the sets separately from scratch and evaluate their cross architecture performance
comments.explanation: The cross-architecture evaluation is a key contribution, but implementation details are underspecified. It is unclear whether synthetic sets learned with one architecture (e.g., ResNet) are averaged over multiple initializations (K steps) or used separately per seed. When training target architectures (e.g., MLP on ResNet-condensed images), it is not specified whether hyperparameters (learning rate, steps ρ_θ) are tuned per architecture or reused. The paper mentions Kaiming initialization but does not confirm training adaptability. These choices could affect the strength of generalization claims. While the general procedure is clear, the lack of specification on aggregation and tuning undermines full reproducibility, especially for an experiment central to the paper’s impact.

comments.title: Missing clarification on use of real vs. synthetic initialization in applications
comments.quote: we generate and stored in the memory, keep the rest of the model same and evaluate this model on the task-incremental learning problem on the digit recognition datasets, SVHN (Netzer et al., 2011), MNIST (LeCun et al., 1998) and USPS (Hull, 1994) in the same order.
comments.explanation: In the continual learning experiment, it is not specified whether synthetic images are learned from scratch per task or reused incrementally. Since condensation as described in Section 2 uses full training data (offline), applying it in a continual setting where past data is inaccessible requires adaptation. The paper does not clarify whether the condensation stage C uses only current-task data or assumes full access. It also does not confirm whether C and T use the same architecture. Without this, the experiment risks violating standard continual learning assumptions (e.g., no access to prior data). While likely resolved via task-wise condensation, explicit clarification is necessary for reproducibility and validity of the application.

comments.title: Overstatement of generality in architecture independence of condensed images
comments.quote: we show that these images are not architecture dependent, can be used to train different deep networks.
comments.explanation: The conclusion claims synthetic images are 'not architecture dependent', but the paper's own results show asymmetries: MLP-generated images generalize poorly to convolutional networks due to translation invariance mismatch, while convolutional-generated images improve MLP training. This indicates that condensed images *are* influenced by the generator architecture's inductive biases. The stronger claim overgeneralizes the results and could mislead readers into expecting universal transferability. A more accurate statement would acknowledge that while cross-architecture performance is strong (especially from ResNet-like generators), transfer success varies meaningfully with the generator-classifier pairing.

While context resolves the intended meaning—minibatch gradients are standard—the lack of distinction (e.g., using ℓ for per-batch loss) risks confusing readers about whether full-batch gradients are required. Additionally, label space notation shifts from y ∈ {0, ..., C−1} to y ∈ Y without defining Y, introducing ambiguity about label flexibility. comments.title: Ambiguity in terminology and notation for stages and pipeline
comments.quote: The pipeline for dataset condensation has two stages: learning the condensed images (denoted as C) and training classifiers from scratch on them (denoted as T).
comments.explanation: The use of 'T' to denote the training stage conflicts with its earlier definition as the original training dataset. Similarly, the term 'curriculum' is used to describe stepwise optimization path matching, but this differs from standard curriculum learning (sample difficulty scheduling). The method does not implement a curriculum in sampling or scheduling—only iterative gradient matching—so the term is metaphorical at best. comments.title: Ambiguous interpretation of 'relative accuracy' and normalization in Figure 3
comments.quote: relative terms – normalized by its upper-bound. Increasing the number of condensed images improves the accuracies in all benchmarks and further closes the gap with the upper-bound performance especially in MNIST and FashionMNIST, while the gap remains larger in SVHN and CIFAR10.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'normalized by its upper-bound' suggests the y-axis shows accuracy divided by full-dataset accuracy (e.g., ratio scaled to %). However, the plotted values (e.g., 94.8%, 95.2%) closely match absolute full-data accuracies from other sections, implying 'relative accuracy' actually means 'absolute accuracy as a percentage of full-data performance' (i.e., performance ratio). The ambiguity arises because 'relative accuracy' is used inconsistently in literature. Clarifying that this is 'performance relative to full-data training (as percentage)' would prevent misinterpretation. While context resolves it, the initial phrasing is imprecise.

comments.title: Underspecified label handling and scalability of synthetic data
comments.quote: we learn to synthesize images for fixed labels, e.g. one synthetic image per class
comments.explanation: The method uses fixed, per-class labels rather than jointly optimizing labels. While practical, this choice is not experimentally justified—no ablation compares fixed vs. learned labels. More importantly, scalability to high-class datasets like ImageNet (1000 classes) is questionable if at least one synthetic image per class is required. The paper uses 1–50 images per class, suggesting difficulty with extreme compression. This limitation is not framed as a constraint but as a design choice, potentially misleading readers about the method’s compressibility potential.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated causal claim about gradient flow and architecture choices
comments.quote: leaky ReLU over ReLU and average pooling over max pooling enable learning better condensed images, as they allow for denser gradient flow
comments.explanation: The authors attribute improved performance to 'denser gradient flow' from leaky ReLU and average pooling. While plausible—leaky ReLU avoids dead neurons and average pooling is fully differentiable—this mechanistic explanation lacks empirical support. No ablation, gradient sparsity analysis, or control experiment validates this causal claim. In deep learning research, such interpretations require additional evidence before being presented as conclusions. The finding that these components work better may be valid, but the explanation goes beyond what the data supports.

comments.title: Unjustified efficiency claims without experimental anchoring
comments.quote: Finally our method trains 2 times faster than DD and requires 50% less memory on CIFAR10 experiments.
comments.explanation: The claim of 2× speedup and 50% memory reduction lacks supporting details in the main text. Without specifying computational setup (e.g., unrolling depth in DD, optimizer steps, batch sizes), the numbers appear definitive but are unanchored. While the method design (avoiding unrolled optimization) plausibly enables gains, specific figures should be tied to experiments (e.g., 'as in Table 5') for credibility. As stated, the claim edges toward mild overstatement, despite likely being accurate.

comments.title: Ambiguous synthetic image initialization in experiments
comments.quote: The synthetic images can be initialized from Gaussian noise or randomly selected real training images.
comments.explanation: The paper states two initialization options but does not specify which was used in the reported experiments. Initialization can significantly impact convergence and final performance in gradient-based synthesis. While supplementary material may resolve this, the omission in the main experimental setup limits reproducibility and transparency, especially for a method sensitive to starting conditions.

comments.title: Inadequate discussion of scalability challenges to ImageNet
comments.quote: we plan to explore the use of condensed images in more diverse and thus challenging datasets like ImageNet
comments.explanation: While extending to ImageNet is a natural next step, the statement does not engage with known scalability barriers: increased gradient variance, larger model capacity, complex feature hierarchies, and higher-resolution inputs. The current method uses small models and datasets; transferring to ImageNet-scale condensation is non-trivial. Without acknowledging these challenges or proposing adaptations (e.g., layer-wise condensation, pretraining), the forward-looking claim risks sounding naive about practical feasibility.

The gradients from single-class batches may carry class-specific bias (e.g., class imbalance, feature co-activation patterns), and matching _∇_**θ** L[S]_ to such biased gradients could hinder generalization. While the authors note this design choice helps 'imitating the mean gradients w.r.t. the data from single class', they do not justify why such per-class gradient alignment suffices to recover the full multi-class training dynamics. One might expect that matching gradients over balanced, mixed-class batches would be more representative. The lack of discussion around this—e.g., a comparison between single-class vs. mixed-class gradient matching in the supplementary—leaves a key modeling choice underspecified and potentially misleading for replication.

comments.title: Unjustified simplification in gradient matching derivation
comments.quote: Based on our observation ( _D_ ( _**θ** t[S][,]_ _**[ θ]** t[T]_[)] _[ ≈]_[0][), we simplify the formula-] tion in eq. (7) by replacing _**θ** t[T]_ with _**θ** t[S]_ and use _**θ**_ to denote _**θ**[S]_ in the rest of the paper
comments.explanation: The transition from parameter-space matching to gradient-space matching hinges on the assumption that _**θ** t[S] ≈ **θ** t[T]_, which the authors claim is supported by preliminary experiments. However, this is a circular argument: the observation presupposes the success of the method before deriving it. The simplification in Eq. (9) effectively collapses _∇_**θ** L[T]_( _**θ** t[T]_) to _∇_**θ** L[T]_( _**θ** t[S]_), relying on parameter proximity and loss smoothness. While plausible under smoothness assumptions, deriving gradient matching from this requires a first-order Taylor expansion or Lipschitz assumption on the Hessian, which is not provided. This is a non-trivial leap from parameter convergence to gradient alignment. The claim 'our goal reduces to matching the gradients' is thus insufficiently derived and risks misrepresenting the conditions under which gradient matching is valid. A careful reader might conclude that gradient matching is proposed as a heuristic rather than a consequence.

comments.title: Inconsistent gradient source in loss decomposition
comments.quote: The matching loss can be decomposed into a sum of layerwise losses as _D_ ( _∇_ _**θ** L[S] , ∇_ _**θ** L[T]_ ) =[] _[L] l_ =1 _[d]_[(] _[∇]_ _**θ**_[(] _[l]_[)] _[L][S][,][ ∇]_ _**θ**_[(] _[l]_[)] _[L][T]_[ )][
comments.explanation: The notation _∇_**θ**(l)[L[S]]_ suggests the gradient of the loss w.r.t. the weights of layer _l_, which is standard. However, both _L[T]_ and _L[S]_ are scalar losses computed on different datasets, and their gradients are evaluated at the current parameter state _**θ**_ _t_. The decomposition is reasonable, but confusion arises because _L[T]_ and _L[S]_ are not functions of each other, and their gradients are computed independently. The text implies they are compared layer-by-layer, but does not clarify whether the gradient matching is done between _∇_**θ**(l)[L[T]_( _**θ** t)]_ and _∇_**θ**(l)[L[S]_( _**θ** t)]_—which would be standard—or if there’s a mismatch in evaluation points. Given the method uses the same _**θ**_ _t_ for both forward passes (line 7 in Algorithm 1), this is likely correct. OCR may have caused formatting issues (e.g., '[]' for summation), but the core idea is consistent with the algorithm. Thus, while the notation is slightly ambiguous, context resolves it.

comments.title: Overstatement of generality in multi-architecture training
comments.quote: learn a single set of synthetic images that can be used to train different neural network architectures
comments.explanation: The abstract and introduction pose this as a core question and imply the method achieves it. However, the experiments in Section 3.1 evaluate performance across architectures *using synthetically condensed sets learned with one architecture at a time* (e.g., ConvNet used to condense, then tested on ResNet). The paper does not demonstrate that a *single* synthetic set is effective across *all* architectures (e.g., one set learned using a ConvNet that simultaneously trains MLP, ResNet, and VGG to high accuracy). Such cross-architecture generalization would be a much stronger claim. Instead, results in Table 1 show per-architecture performance using synthetically trained models, but the condensation process itself may be architecture-dependent. The text risks implying broader transferability than is demonstrated, especially to readers skimming the abstract. This is not a methodological error, but a framing overstatement.

comments.title: Underspecified label handling in synthetic data
comments.quote: we learn to synthesize images for fixed labels, _e.g_ . one synthetic image per class
comments.explanation: The discussion admits that joint optimization of labels and images is challenging and opts for fixed-per-class labeling. While practical, this choice is not experimentally justified—e.g., there’s no ablation showing whether learned labels (e.g., via soft labels or label optimization) hurt or help. More importantly, the method's scalability to datasets with many classes (e.g., ImageNet with 1000 classes) is questionable if it requires at least one synthetic image per class. The paper uses only 1–50 images per class (Table 1), suggesting potential difficulty with extreme compression. This doesn't invalidate the method but underspecifies a key constraint: the fixed label assumption limits flexibility and may prevent higher compression rates. A reader might miss that this is a critical design limitation rather than a minor choice.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for dataset size in loss function definition
comments.explanation: At first glance, the expression '|T| 1' appears to be a typo—likely intended to be '1/|T|' to denote averaging the loss over the dataset. The current rendering suggests multiplication by |T| and then 1, which would scale the loss upward, contrary to standard empirical risk minimization where the average loss is minimized. This is a common normalization in machine learning, so the reverse would be highly unusual. Given that OCR errors often corrupt fractions (e.g., '1/|T|' misread as '|T| 1'), and the context clearly aims to define an empirical loss (as stated in the surrounding prose), it is highly likely this is an OCR-induced inversion. The subsequent use of L[S] with '|S| 1' confirms the pattern, suggesting both are meant to be 1/|T| and 1/|S| respectively. While the surrounding discussion and standard conventions resolve the intended meaning, the notation as written is technically incorrect and could mislead a careful reader unfamiliar with typical empirical loss formulations.

comments.title: Ambiguous decomposition of parameter matching into iterative subproblems
comments.quote:
We can now decompose eq. (5) into multiple subproblems:

where _T_ is the number of iterations, _ς[S]_ and _ς[T]_ are the numbers of optimization steps for _**θ**[S]_ and _**θ**[T]_ respectively.
comments.explanation: The text introduces a decomposition of the parameter matching objective (eq. 5) into iterative subproblems involving _**θ** t[S]_ and _**θ** t[T]_, but no explicit equation is provided for this decomposition—only prose follows. This creates ambiguity about how exactly eq. (5) is broken down. Are the subproblems minimizing D(_**θ** t[S], _**θ** t[T]) at each step t and summing over t? Is it a sequence of matching conditions enforced during optimization? The lack of a formal equation makes it difficult to reconstruct the optimization objective precisely. While the authors later describe gradient matching as an approximation to this (leading into eq. 9), the logical bridge from parameter matching (eq. 5) to the iterative formulation (eq. 6, which is missing) is underspecified. This gap is partially resolved by the subsequent explanation of gradient matching, but the intermediate conceptual step remains ambiguous, especially for readers trying to understand the motivation behind gradient matching. A clear equation for the decomposition would have made the progression more rigorous.

comments.title: Unjustified simplification from parameter difference to gradient matching
comments.quote: Based on our observation ( _D_ ( _**θ** t[S][,]_ _**[ θ]** t[T]_[)] _[ ≈]_[0][), we simplify the formula-tion in eq. (7) by replacing _**θ** t[T]_[with] _**[ θ]** t[S]_[and use] _**[ θ]**_[ to denote] _**[ θ]**[S]_[in the rest of the paper:]
comments.explanation: The authors observe that D(_**θ** t[S], _**θ** t[T]) ≈ 0 and use this to justify replacing _**θ** t[T]_ with _**θ** t[S]_ in the update rule (eq. 8), leading directly to gradient matching. However, this leap lacks sufficient mathematical justification. The small *parameter* difference does not directly imply that matching *gradients* suffices—this depends on the local Lipschitz continuity of the gradient or higher-order smoothness assumptions not discussed. While it is intuitive that similar parameters lead to similar gradients for smooth losses, this is a non-trivial approximation that should be explicitly acknowledged. Moreover, replacing _**θ** t[T]_ with _**θ** t[S]_ in the gradient expression leads to _∇_ **θ** L[T](**θ**) and _∇_ **θ** L[S](**θ**) being compared at the same **θ**, which forms the basis of gradient matching—but this step is presented as a simple substitution rather than a meaningful approximation with potential implications. In a methodological paper, this derivation should be made more rigorous or at least accompanied by a discussion of the approximation error.

comments.title: Mismatch between label space notation in data and synthetic set definitions
comments.quote: _y_ ∈ {0, . . . , C − 1}_,] _[ X]_[is a d-dimensional input space and] _C_ is the number of classes. Our goal is to generate a small set of condensed synthetic samples with their labels, _S_ = _{_ ( _**s** i, yi_ ) _}|[|S|] i_ =1[where] _**[ s]**[∈]_[R] _[d]_[and] _[ y][∈Y]_
comments.explanation: The original dataset defines labels as y ∈ {0, ..., C−1}, a standard discrete label space. However, for the synthetic set S, the label space is abruptly changed to y ∈ Y without defining Y. Given that Y is not defined elsewhere in the provided context, this creates a notation inconsistency. Is Y meant to be the same as {0, ..., C−1}? Or does it allow for soft labels or continuous embeddings? The surrounding context (e.g., one synthetic image per class) suggests hard class labels are used, so Y should equal the original label space. The use of Y may be intentional to allow for future generalization, but without clarification, it introduces ambiguity. This is especially relevant since the discussion mentions learning labels jointly with samples 'in theory', suggesting Y might differ. However, as labels are kept fixed in experiments, the discrepancy has no practical effect. Still, for clarity, Y should be explicitly equated to {0, ..., C−1} or motivated otherwise.

comments.title: Underspecified 'curriculum' aspect of gradient matching
comments.quote: Here we propose a curriculum based approach to address the above mentioned challenges. The key idea is that we wish _**θ**[S]_ to be close to not only the final _**θ**[T]_ but also to follow a similar path to _**θ**[T]_ throughout the optimization.
comments.explanation: The term 'curriculum' is used to describe matching optimization trajectories, but this does not align with the standard meaning of curriculum learning, which involves ordering training samples by difficulty. Here, the 'curriculum' refers to stepwise parameter matching across optimization time, which is more accurately described as *optimization path imitation* or *sequential distillation*. While the core idea is sound—matching intermediate parameters to guide learning—the use of 'curriculum' is misleading without qualification. Furthermore, the method does not actually use a curriculum in the synthetic data sampling or optimization scheduling. The term appears metaphorical, but without clarification, it risks conceptual conflation. The algorithm itself (Alg. 1) does not implement any curriculum in sample difficulty or learning progression, only iterative gradient matching. This is a minor framing issue, as the technical content is correct, but the terminology could confuse readers expecting a pedagogical curriculum.

comments.title: Ambiguous Gradient Matching Loss Decomposition
comments.quote:
The matching loss can be decomposed into a sum of layerwise losses as _D_ ( _∇_ _**θ** L[S] , ∇_ _**θ** L[T]_ ) =[] _[L] l_ =1 _[d]_[(] _[∇]_ _**θ**_[(] _[l]_[)] _[L][S][,][ ∇]_ _**θ**_[(] _[l]_[)] _[L][T]_[ )][ where] _[ l]_[ is the layer index,] _[ L]_[ is the number of] layers with weights and

where **A** _i·_ and **B** _i·_ are flattened vectors of gradients corresponding to each output node _i_ , which is in dimensional for FC weights and in _×_ h _×_ w dimensional for convolutional weights.
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned about the transition from the layerwise decomposition to the per-output-node grouping. The equation fragment _D_ ( _∇_ _**θ** L[S], ∇_ _**θ** L[T] ) = [] _[L] l=1 [d]_(∇_**θ**_[l]_ L[S], ∇_**θ**_[l]_ L[T]) suggests a sum over layers of some distance d on gradients per layer, which is standard. But the following sentence introducing **A**_i·_ and **B**_i·_ as flattened vectors 'corresponding to each output node i' implies a finer decomposition—per-output-channel rather than per-layer—and the distance d(·,·) is never formally defined. The description suggests d aggregates gradients per output node across input dimensions (in × h × w), which is reasonable and aligns with the authors’ experimental claim of better performance with a single learning rate. However, the abrupt shift from layerwise to node-wise without a formal definition of d or the link between the two forms is jarring. While it’s plausible that d(∇_**θ**_[l]_ L[S], ∇_**θ**_[l]_ L[T]) = ∑_i d_node(**A**_i·_, **B**_i·_), this is not stated. Given that they cite improvements over methods that flatten all gradients, their design choice makes sense. But the lack of a precise formula—especially given OCR artifacts like '[]' and '[L]' possibly replacing '∑' and 'l'—leaves ambiguity about how the sum is structured. This could mislead a reader trying to implement the loss. The supplementary reference is mentioned, but the main paper should minimally define d.

comments.title: Misleading Statement on Recursive Computation Graph Unrolling
comments.quote: This approximation has the key advantage over (Wang et al., 2018) and eq. (5) that it does not require the expensive unrolling of the recursive computation graph over the previous parameters _{_ _**θ**_ 0 _, . . . ,_ _**θ** t−_ 1 _}_ .
comments.explanation: At first glance, this seems accurate: the paper’s gradient matching avoids backpropagating through the optimizer steps used to update **θ**, unlike Dataset Distillation (Wang et al., 2018), which requires differentiating through the inner optimization. However, the phrasing 'recursive computation graph over the previous parameters {**θ**_0, ..., **θ**_{t−1}}' is imprecise. In gradient-based meta-learning methods like DD, the unrolling occurs through optimizer updates—not because the parameters themselves are recursively defined, but because the loss depends on S via **θ**_S(S), which is computed via iterative updates starting from **θ**_0. The paper’s method avoids this by matching gradients at a fixed point (or fixed step) without requiring **θ**_S to be a deep function of S. So the core claim is correct: their method avoids unrolling. But attributing the cost to a 'recursive computation graph over {**θ**_0, ..., **θ**_{t−1}}' mischaracterizes the computational burden as arising from the parameter sequence itself, rather than from the nested optimization dependency. This could mislead readers unfamiliar with implicit differentiation or meta-learning about where the computational cost actually lies. The advantage is real, but the explanation oversimplifies and slightly misrepresents the nature of the unrolling.

comments.title: Underspecified Synthetic Data Update in Algorithm 1
comments.explanation: The algorithm describes updating Sc (synthetic data for class c) based on minimizing the distance between gradients of L[S]_c and L[T]_c w.r.t. **θ**_t. However, the opt-alg_S is described as taking a distance D and performing ςS gradient steps. But D is a loss that depends on **θ**, which is fixed at **θ**_t during this update. So opt-alg_S should be performing gradient descent on S_c via ∇_{S_c} D(∇_**θ** L[S]_c(**θ**_t), ∇_**θ** L[T]_c(**θ**_t)). The text says 'We use the stochastic gradient descent optimization for both opt-alg_**θ** and opt-alg_S', so this is likely the case. However, the notation 'opt-alg_S( D(...), ςS, ηS )' treats D as the objective, which is correct, but it's unclear whether the gradient computation ∇_{S} D is computed and applied correctly, especially since D involves gradients of **θ**, creating a higher-order derivative (Hessian-like term w.r.t. mixed variables). The omission of ∇_{S} in the call is a notational shorthand, but given that this is a core and non-trivial computational step (involving ∇_{S}∇_**θ** L[S]_c), it should be at least acknowledged. While the overall procedure is described, a reader attempting to implement might not realize the need for second-order gradients with respect to S unless they trace the chain rule carefully. This is a subtle but real implementation barrier.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of Loss Notation in Algorithm 1
comments.explanation: The text defines L[T] and L[S] as full dataset losses, e.g., L_T(θ) = 1/|T| ∑_{(x,y)∈T} ℓ(φ_θ(x), y). But here, L[T]_c and L[S]_c are computed over minibatches Bc[T] and Bc[S]. This is fine algorithmically—minibatch gradients are standard—but the notation overloads L with two meanings: sometimes as the full empirical loss, sometimes as a per-minibatch loss. The subscript c further indicates a class-specific batch. While common in practice, this shift from population to batch losses is not clarified. Since the gradient matching in Eq. (9) and Algorithm 1 is based on minibatch gradients, it's essential that L[T]_c and L[S]_c are indeed minibatch losses. But the potential confusion arises because earlier in the paper (e.g., Eq. (1), (2)) L[T](θ) is defined over the full T. Context resolves this: the algorithm is stochastic, so minibatch usage is expected. However, for a careful reader, the lack of explicit distinction—e.g., using ℓ instead of L for per-sample or per-batch loss—could cause momentary confusion about whether the method requires full-batch gradients. The context and algorithm loop structure justify the minibatch use, so this is not a critical flaw, but better notation (e.g., ℓ_batch or explicit E[∇]) would improve clarity.

comments.title: Ambiguity in synthetic image initialization options
comments.quote: The synthetic images can be initialized from Gaussian noise or randomly selected real training images.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this sentence appears straightforward, stating two options for initializing synthetic images. However, it raises a concern: does the choice between Gaussian noise and real image initialization significantly affect performance? More importantly, does the paper clearly indicate which initialization method was used in the reported experiments? Given that the ablation of initialization strategy could impact results—especially in gradient-based synthesis methods—the lack of specification in the main text is ambiguous. While supplementary material may resolve this, the main paper should ideally state which choice was used in the experiments. The context shows that results are reported in Table 1 and others, but no mention is made of which initialization was employed. In high-impact methods like dataset condensation, initialization can influence convergence and final image quality. Therefore, omitting this detail in the experimental setup limits reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent formatting in Table 3 and duplicated content
comments.explanation:
The passage presents a block of text that appears to be a corrupted or duplicated rendering of Table 3, likely due to OCR or formatting error during PDF extraction. The table content is repeated twice, and the second instance is malformed (e.g., '10
36.8_±_1.2 **39.1**_±_**1.2**|83.1_±_0.2|||'). While this is almost certainly a document formatting issue rather than an author error, it affects readability and raises concern about data integrity. However, from context, the intended content is clear: for MNIST with 10 imgs/cls, Ours achieves 93.9%, DD 79.5%; for CIFAR10 with 10 imgs/cls, Ours 39.1%, DD 36.8%. These values are consistent with the prose ('Our method achieves significantly better performance than DD'). Since this appears to be an artifact of document conversion and not a flaw in the original paper, it does not reflect poorly on the authors’ rigor.

comments.title: Missing clarification on architecture mismatch in cross-architecture evaluation
comments.quote: Note that the model architectures used in two stages might be different.
comments.explanation: This sentence correctly anticipates that condensed data trained on one architecture may be used to train another—a key claim of the paper's broader applicability. However, the sentence appears after the description of the evaluation protocol involving 5 synthetic sets and 20 models each, without explicitly stating whether architecture differences were actually used in Table 1 or Table 2. Table 2 clearly shows cross-architecture results (e.g., synthetic data trained with ConvNet used to train MLP), but the surrounding text does not emphasize this connection. A careful reader might initially miss that Table 2 is the empirical validation of this claim. While not technically incorrect, the logical flow could be improved to directly link the textual statement to Table 2. That said, the table itself supports the claim, and experts would infer the correspondence. Thus, this is a minor issue of clarity rather than a methodological gap.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'pipeline has two stages' and potential conflation of roles
comments.quote: The pipeline for dataset condensation has two stages: learning the condensed images (denoted as C) and training classifiers from scratch on them (denoted as T).
comments.explanation: At first, the denoting of stages as 'C' and 'T' seemed puzzling: these symbols were previously used for condensed dataset (S), original dataset (T), and network (φ_θ). Here, 'T' is reused to denote the second stage (training), which conflicts with earlier use of T as the original dataset. This is a notational overloading. However, upon closer reading, the authors use 'T' here in a meta-level description of stages, not within equations. The label 'T' for the training stage is not referenced again in any equation or algorithm, so the conflict is only in prose and not in formal notation. The context prevents confusion because T as dataset is always in math mode or clearly defined, while here it's in a descriptive sentence. Still, using 'T' for training stage alongside T as training set risks confusion, especially for a new reader. A different letter (e.g., 'F' for final training) would have been safer.

comments.title: Unjustified claim about training speed and memory savings vs DD
comments.quote: Finally our method trains 2 times faster than DD and requires 50% less memory on CIFAR10 experiments.
comments.explanation: This claim about efficiency improvements is valuable, but it lacks justification in the main text. The reader is told these numbers without access to computational setup details (e.g., optimizer steps, unrolling depth in DD, batch sizes). While the supplementary may contain this, the core advantage of gradient matching—avoiding unrolled optimization—is presented earlier as the reason for scalability. Given that DD requires unrolling over inner-loop steps (which becomes expensive), and the proposed method avoids it via gradient matching, a 2× speedup seems plausible. However, without citing a source or experiment identifier (e.g., 'as shown in Table 5'), this statement feels under-supported. It is not a major flaw because the method design inherently avoids expensive second-order computations, but the specific numbers (2×, 50%) are presented as definitive without anchoring them in the presented results. This edges toward mild overclaim, though likely accurate.

comments.title: Ambiguous interpretation of 'normalized by its upper-bound' in Figure 3 caption
comments.quote: relative terms – normalized by its upper-bound. Increasing the number of condensed images improves the accuracies in all benchmarks and further closes the gap with the upper-bound performance especially in MNIST and FashionMNIST, while the gap remains larger in SVHN and CIFAR10.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the phrase 'normalized by its upper-bound' suggests that the y-axis in the 'Relative accuracy (%)' subplots of Figure 3 shows accuracy divided by the full-dataset accuracy (e.g., a ratio between 0 and 1, scaled to percent). This would make sense as a way to compare performance across datasets. However, the actual values plotted (e.g., 94.8%, 95.2%, 93.0%, etc.) are very close to the absolute full-dataset accuracy values reported elsewhere (e.g., MNIST: 99.6%, FashionMNIST: 93.5%, SVHN: 95.4%, CIFAR10: 84.8%). This implies that 'relative accuracy' likely means 'absolute accuracy as a percentage of the full-dataset accuracy' (i.e., relative performance ratio), not that the raw accuracies were normalized and rescaled. While careful readers can infer this from context (Table 1 and text), the phrasing risks misleading someone into thinking the plot shows raw normalized scores rather than performance ratios. The term 'relative accuracy' is ambiguous in machine learning literature — sometimes it refers to differences, sometimes to ratios. Clarifying that this is 'performance relative to full-data training (as a percentage)' would eliminate ambiguity. The context eventually resolves this, but the initial confusion arises from imprecise terminology.

comments.title: Potential inconsistency in ResNet-18 results across tables and text
comments.quote: The best results are obtained in most cases with ResNet generated images and ConvNet or ResNet as classifiers which is inline with their performances when trained on the original dataset.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that 'the best results are obtained in most cases with ResNet generated images' in the cross-architecture evaluation on MNIST (Section 3.1). However, Table 2 — which is referenced but not fully shown in the provided context — must contain detailed cross-architecture results to support this. Since Table 2 is not included in the provided text, we cannot verify the claim. But the passage asserts this conclusion with confidence, implying ResNet-synthesized images generalize best across architectures. This may conflict with intuitive expectations: if models are trained from random initialization, and the distilled data was generated using ResNet’s complex inductive biases, it might overfit to those features. The authors’ reasoning ties the result to original-dataset performance ('inline with their performances'), suggesting that stronger models generate better data. While this is plausible, the claim assumes Table 2 supports this broad conclusion. If ResNet-generated images only win in a narrow setting (e.g., only when the classifier is also ResNet), the generalization claim weakens. Given the key role of cross-architecture generalization in promoting the method’s utility, the lack of quantitative support in the provided context raises concern. Since Table 2 presumably contains the evidence, and assuming its data is accurate, the issue is minor — but the text overstates generality without qualifying which 'most cases' show this pattern.

comments.title: Missing clarification on use of real vs. synthetic initialization in applications
comments.quote: we generate and stored in the memory, keep the rest of the model same and evaluate this model on the task-incremental learning problem on the digit recognition datasets, SVHN (Netzer et al., 2011), MNIST (LeCun et al., 1998) and USPS (Hull, 1994) in the same order.
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the continual learning experiment replaces herding with dataset condensation in the E2E framework. However, it does not specify whether the synthetic images are learned from scratch at each task or reused incrementally. Earlier, synthetic images are learned using full training data (offline), but continual learning assumes data from previous tasks is not accessible. The method's success depends on whether the condensation process is applied only to the current task’s data (plausible) or somehow uses the full stream (unrealistic). Additionally, it is not clarified whether the condensation stage C uses the same architecture as the training classifier. The paper states earlier that C and T may differ, but in NAS it notes that condensed images are trained once with default ConvNet and reused. For continual learning, it should specify whether a new set of synthetic images is distilled per task using only that task’s data — otherwise, it risks appearing to use future or full data, which violates continual learning assumptions. This is not flagged as a major error because the method description in Section 2 emphasizes learning from T (full data), but in application, this must be adapted. The omission leaves the continual learning setup underspecified, though likely resolvable from standard practice (i.e., task-wise condensation). Still, given the significance of the claim, explicitly stating the adaptation would improve reproducibility.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about gradient flow properties of leaky ReLU and average pooling
comments.quote: leaky ReLU over ReLU and average pooling over max pooling enable learning better condensed images, as they allow for denser gradient flow
comments.explanation: The authors attribute the superior performance of leaky ReLU and average pooling to 'denser gradient flow'. This explanation is plausible — leaky ReLU avoids dying neurons by allowing small negative gradients, and average pooling is fully differentiable everywhere (unlike max pooling, which routes gradients only through argmax units). However, the paper provides no empirical evidence or ablation to support this causal interpretation. While the observation (that these choices yield better results) may be valid, asserting a mechanistic explanation without justification — such as gradient variance analysis, sparsity metrics, or control experiments — is speculative. In deep learning research, such claims often require additional validation before being presented as conclusions. The authors are correct that these components affect gradient propagation, but linking this directly to improved condensation success is a stronger claim than the data strictly supports. The supplementary is referenced, but unless it contains such analysis, this remains an unjustified causal assertion. Given that this insight might guide future method design, the lack of support is noteworthy but doesn’t invalidate the empirical finding.

comments.title: Missing details on how cross-architecture evaluation is trained
comments.quote: Once the condensed sets are synthesized, we train every network on all the sets separately from scratch and evaluate their cross architecture performance
comments.explanation: The cross-architecture experiment is a key demonstration of generalization: synthetic images learned with one architecture (e.g., ResNet) are used to train another (e.g., MLP). However, the passage does not specify critical implementation details: Are the condensed images learned using a fixed architecture and then tested on others? Is there one shared synthetic set per source architecture, or are multiple sets learned across initializations? The method in Section 2.3 involves averaging over multiple random initializations (K steps). If the synthetic set S is learned using ResNet with K different initializations, is the final S averaged? Or is one S used per seed? This affects reproducibility. Moreover, when training target architectures (e.g., training an MLP on ResNet-condensed images), are the hyperparameters (learning rate, steps ς_θ) tuned per architecture, or shared? The paper states Kaiming initialization is used, but does not confirm whether optimizer settings are adapted. While the general procedure is clear, the lack of specification on how S is aggregated and whether training is fairly tuned per classifier introduces ambiguity. The claim of strong generalization could be sensitive to these choices. This is not a fatal flaw — the trend may hold — but it qualifies as underspecified for full replication.

comments.title: Ambiguous claim about architecture independence of condensed images
comments.quote: we show that these images are not architecture dependent, can be used to train different deep networks.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this claim appears overstated given the nuanced results in the paper. The authors do show cross-architecture generalization (e.g., ResNet-generated images performing well on other architectures), but they also observe asymmetries: MLP-generated images generalize poorly to convolutional networks due to translation invariance mismatch, while convolutional-generated images improve MLP performance. This suggests that condensed images are *not fully* architecture-independent — their quality and transferability depend significantly on the generator architecture. The stronger claim in the conclusion, taken literally, could mislead a reader into thinking any condensed set works equally well across all architectures, which is contradicted by earlier findings. While the authors deserve credit for demonstrating impressive cross-architecture utility (especially ResNet → others), the conclusion overgeneralizes this into a universal property. A more precise statement would acknowledge that while condensed images are surprisingly transferable, performance varies meaningfully with generator and classifier architecture pairing.

comments.title: Misleading implication about memory footprint reduction without qualification
comments.quote: Once trained, they can be used to lower the memory print of datasets
comments.explanation: The term 'memory print' (likely meant as 'footprint') suggests a direct storage saving, which is true for the *condensed set itself*. However, this statement does not clarify that the original dataset is still required during the condensation phase — which is computationally and memory-intensive. The reader might infer that the method eliminates the need for storing large datasets altogether, but in reality, the full dataset must be available to learn the synthetic images in the first place. The final synthetic set *can* replace the original for downstream training, but the condensation process does not eliminate reliance on the full dataset initially. This is a subtle but important distinction, especially for applications where data privacy or access is constrained. The authors correctly position the benefit in later sections, but this sentence, without context, may overstate the immediacy of memory savings.

comments.title: Inadequate discussion of scalability to ImageNet in future work
comments.quote: we plan to explore the use of condensed images in more diverse and thus challenging datasets like ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009) that contain higher resolution images with larger variations in appearance and pose of objects, background.
comments.explanation: While mentioning ImageNet as future work is reasonable, the statement lacks engagement with known scalability challenges discussed in the field. Condensation methods based on gradient matching become dramatically more difficult at ImageNet scale due to increased variance in gradients, greater model capacity needs, and more complex feature hierarchies. The paper uses relatively small models (e.g., ResNet-18) and simple datasets; jumping to ImageNet implies a vast increase in difficulty. Moreover, the assumed initialization (e.g., from scratch) and optimization dynamics may not transfer. The authors have done solid work on smaller benchmarks, but this forward-looking statement does not acknowledge these hurdles. A stronger position would temper expectations or propose specific adaptations (e.g., layer-wise condensation, or using pretrained features). As written, it risks sounding naive about the practical barriers.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper introduces a compelling and well-motivated framework for "dataset condensation," effectively addressing the computational burden of large-scale deep learning by reformulating data selection as a gradient-matching optimization problem. The writing is highly clear, and the authors demonstrate professional acumen by situating their work rigorously within the existing literature on coreset construction, dataset distillation, and generative modeling. The transition from the limitations of heuristic-based selection to their proposed learning-based approach is logical and persuasive, providing a strong narrative foundation for the empirical results that follow. If there is a potential oversight, it lies in the ambitious scope; by aiming to solve dataset compression, continual learning, and neural architecture search simultaneously, the authors must ensure that the depth of the theoretical analysis—specifically regarding the convergence properties of gradient matching—is not sacrificed in favor of breadth. Overall, the introduction sets the stage for a high-impact contribution by clearly defining the problem, the methodology, and the significant practical advantages over the current state-of-the-art.

comments.title: Unclear aggregation of per-class gradient matching
comments.quote: Note that we sample each real and synthetic batch pair from T and S containing samples from a single class
comments.explanation: The paper describes computing gradient matching loss per class to reduce memory, but fails to define how these per-class objective components are aggregated into a global loss. Specifically, it is unclear if the matching is performed against the global gradient or independent per-class gradients, and how the network architecture's parameter dependencies (e.g., shared layers) are handled when only a subset of neurons is active for a given class minibatch.

comments.title: Logical gap in derivation of gradient matching objective
comments.quote: Based on our observation (D(θtS, θtT) ≈ 0), we simplify the formulation in eq. (7) by replacing θtT with θtS
comments.explanation: The transition from a trajectory-matching objective to a gradient-matching objective is presented as a 'simplification' based on the observation that the parameter distance is near zero. However, this lacks a formal derivation; if the parameters are already identical, the trajectory-matching objective essentially vanishes. The paper justifies replacing parameter-matching with gradient-matching as a heuristic, but this leap ignores the path-dependency inherent in the original objective and requires clearer justification of why per-step gradient alignment serves as a surrogate for long-term trajectory matching.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of optimization objective for S
comments.quote: Update S_c ← opt-alg_S ( D ( ∇_θ L_S_c ( θ_t ) , ∇_θ L_T_c ( θ_t ) ) , ς_S , η_S )
comments.explanation: The notation 'opt-alg_S(D(...))' is underspecified. It is unclear if 'opt-alg' is performing backpropagation through the distance function D with respect to the images in S, or if it represents a different optimization primitive. Given the reliance on the gradient matching approach, the inputs and the functional dependence of S on D should be explicitly mapped for clarity.

comments.title: Inconsistent and broken notation for gradient dimensionality
